01

About

I'm a physics engineer turned nanotechnologist with a hands-on command of the full semiconductor pipeline: design → fabrication → characterization. My master's work focused on process enhancement of amorphous-silicon single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs), spanning photolithography, RIE and wet etching, mesa fabrication, and electro-optical characterization.

Before that I founded and led New Energy, a renewable-energy company, where I won international tenders in competitive bidding and managed each one from the first page of the bid to final acceptance — technical structuring of the tendered items, provisioning and procurement, cross-border logistics, on-site installation, and the in-country and international teams that delivered it. That blend of technical depth and end-to-end ownership is what I bring: I can take a device from a simulation idea through cleanroom realization to a characterized, working result — and I can run the program around it.

I'm currently seeking roles in semiconductor R&D / process engineering, photonics and THz/quantum devices, and systems-engineering or technical program roles where rigorous experimental work meets real-world delivery.

05

Featured Research — Amorphous-Silicon SPADs

M.Sc. thesis & project work: one of the few systematic studies of hydrogenated amorphous-silicon (a-Si:H) single-photon avalanche diodes — from device simulation through cleanroom fabrication to full electro-optical characterization.

Almost all commercial SPADs use crystalline silicon in costly CMOS processes. Amorphous silicon can instead be deposited on arbitrary substrates at low temperature — enabling Thin-Film-on-ASIC (TFA) sensors with near-100% fill factor and electronics placed underneath the detector. The trade-off is a material with more defects and lower carrier mobility, and its single-photon behavior was largely uncharted.

I fabricated four TCO – p – i – n – TCO mesa devices (three a-Si:H, one a-Si:C:H) using two transparent-conductive-oxide anodes — AZO and ITO — then characterized each by I–V, temperature-dependent I–V, spectral response and dark-count measurements to isolate how i-layer thickness, doping and deposition vacuum govern performance.

Custom probe station with integrated hot plate, used for temperature-dependent I–V measurements.

⚛ Interactive — fire a photon at the diode

A single photon absorbed in the i-layer creates an electron–hole pair; the reverse-bias field accelerates the carriers until impact ionization multiplies them into a measurable avalanche. Click anywhere on the device to fire a photon — and watch for spontaneous dark counts, the noise source I measured as DCR.
counts: 0 · dark: 0
Simplified Geiger-mode visualization of the TCO–p-i-n–TCO stack — electrons (cyan) drift to the cathode, holes (orange) to the anode.

DCR

The four devices, compared

Devicei-layerDoping / vacuumTCO · substrate Ideality nEa (eV)Op. windowSpectral peak
SPAD1324 nmLow, high vacuumAZO · Si1.130.50~5 V500 nm · visible
SPAD2125 nm (C-doped)MediumITO · glass1.560.04~0.25 V400 nm · blue
SPAD3140 nmHigh, no vacuumITO · glass1.710.03~4 V420 nm · blue
SPAD41000 nmLowAZO · Si1.13—~15 V560 nm · resp. to 750 nm

Ideality factor (n) and activation energy (Ea) extracted from forward-bias and Arrhenius analysis. SPAD1 — high base vacuum (8×10⁻⁸ Torr), low doping — shows the best junction quality (n→1, highest Ea); SPAD3 — no base vacuum, highest doping — the most defects.

Measured results

Reverse-bias I–V, dark vs. illuminated (semi-log).
Normalised spectral response, all devices.
Arrhenius fits → activation energy per device.

What it showed

Thickness tunes the spectrum

Thick i-layers red-shift the response — SPAD4 (1 µm) peaks at 560 nm, holds >50% response out to 650 nm and cuts off near 750 nm. Thin layers favor UV/blue. A directly usable design knob.

Vacuum & doping set junction quality

High base vacuum + low doping (SPAD1) gave the lowest ideality factor (≈1.13) and highest activation energy (0.50 eV) — i.e. the fewest defects.

A clean thickness–field law

The reverse-bias operation window grew monotonically from 0.25 V (125 nm) to ~15 V (1 µm), matching E = V/d expectations.

A roadmap to Geiger mode

No self-sustained avalanche yet — limited by amorphous low mobility and unterminated edges. Proposed fixes: guard rings, buffer layers, µc/nc-Si:H and optimised PECVD.

Device Simulation — Silvaco TCAD

Preceding fabrication, I modelled a-Si and c-Si SPAD structures in Silvaco TCAD — solving doping profiles, depletion, electric-field distribution and avalanche breakdown to choose the geometry and process targets used in the thesis.

Electric-field distribution across the p-i-n stack.
Simulated avalanche breakdown — cathode current, dark vs. light.
Breakdown voltage vs. i-layer thickness (250 / 300 / 400 nm).

The simulations highlighted edge field-crowding (motivating guard-ring termination) and quantified how i-layer thickness and temperature shift the breakdown voltage — guidance that fed directly into the fabricated-device design.

Bid-to-finalization ownership

Technical structuring & compliance

Translated tender requirements into compliant technical line items — specifying solar-cell and PV-module systems that satisfied both the client's specification and field realities.

Recruited, coordinated and managed in-country and international teams — engineers, suppliers and field crews — keeping distributed projects on schedule.
